facts_disclosed: Never uses chemicals — natural methods only

turns.content: hello friend. I hear on radio today about antibiotic in shrimp farm. government say they find chemical in shrimp from Ben Tre province. what you know about this

Hello! Yes, this is a recurring issue in Vietnamese shrimp farming. Government inspections periodically find antibiotic residues — commonly things like chloramphenicol, enrofloxacin, or oxytetracycline — in shrimp from various provinces including Ben Tre.

The concern is twofold:
1. **Export rejection** — countries like the EU, Japan, and the US have strict limits on antibiotic residues. When they detect them, entire shipments get rejected, which hurts the whole industry's reputation
2. **Consumer health** — antibiotic residue in food contributes to antibiotic resistance, which is a serious public health problem

Some farmers use antibiotics to prevent or treat diseases like white spot syndrome or EMS (Early Mortality Syndrome), especially when stocking densities are high. The problem is that the withdrawal period (time between last antibiotic use and harvest) isn't always followed.

Are you concerned about this for your own operation, or more curious about the broader industry issue?
I am angry about it friend. these farmer make all of us look bad. I have 3 pond, shrimp farm for more than 30 year. I never use chemical in my pond. never

Your frustration is completely understandable. When a few farmers cut corners with chemicals, it damages the reputation of everyone — including responsible farmers like you who have been doing this for decades.

Thirty years of chemical-free farming is genuinely impressive. Many farmers feel the pressure to use antibiotics, especially when disease hits, so maintaining that discipline over such a long time takes real commitment.

The unfortunate reality is that when inspectors find contaminated shrimp, buyers become suspicious of all shrimp from the region — not just the farms that caused the problem. It can drive prices down for everyone.

How do you manage disease prevention without chemicals? Natural methods that work over 30 years are worth understanding.
my father teach me. he farm shrimp before me, same land. he say clean water and good feed is all you need, you know. that is the secret. no secret really. just hard work

Your father sounds like a wise man. And he's right — it's not really a secret, it's just discipline. Clean water management and quality feed are the foundation of healthy shrimp. The basics done well beat shortcuts every time.

There's actually solid science backing this up:
- **Clean water** means lower pathogen load, less stress on the shrimp, stronger immune systems
- **Good feed** means proper nutrition, which helps shrimp resist disease naturally
- **Low stress** shrimp are healthier shrimp — and chemical treatments actually stress them

Many of the disease problems on other farms come from overcrowding, poor water exchange, and cheap feed — then farmers use antibiotics to compensate for bad management instead of fixing the root cause.

Do you do regular water exchanges, or do you manage water quality in other ways?

I change water from canal. my pond connect to canal, I open gate when tide come. but some farmer they put growth hormone too, friend. not just antibiotic. they want shrimp grow fast, big size. but taste is different. natural shrimp taste better, you know

You're absolutely right about the taste difference. Growth hormones and antibiotics can make shrimp grow faster and bigger, but the texture and flavor suffer. Natural shrimp have firmer flesh and a sweeter, cleaner taste — experienced buyers and chefs can tell the difference immediately.

Using tidal water exchange through the canal is a traditional method that works well in the Mekong Delta. The tide brings in fresh, oxygenated water and flushes out waste. It's a natural filtration system.

The growth hormone issue is particularly concerning because:
- It produces shrimp with higher water content (they look big but shrink when cooked)
- The long-term effects on consumers aren't fully understood
- It creates an unfair price competition — those farmers get bigger shrimp faster and can undercut honest farmers at the market

Have you noticed buyers in your area paying more for chemical-free shrimp, or does everyone get the same price regardless?

same price. that is the problem, you know. Binh he pay same whether chemical or no chemical. buyer at market cannot tell from looking. only when they cook they know. so lazy farmer use chemical and get same money but less work

That's the core frustration — there's no market reward for doing the right thing. When buyers can't visually distinguish chemical-free shrimp from treated shrimp, and the price is the same, it actually punishes the honest farmer. You do more work, take more risk with disease, and get paid the same as someone pumping antibiotics.

This is a problem across the industry, but there are some things slowly changing:

1. **Certification programs** — Vietnam has some organic/chemical-free certification schemes (like VietGAP or ASC certification). These let you prove your shrimp are clean and can sometimes command a premium price, especially for export markets

2. **Direct relationships** — some restaurants and retailers specifically seek chemical-free suppliers and will pay more for verified clean product

3. **Testing** — simple antibiotic test strips exist that a buyer could use at point of sale

Have you ever looked into any certification? I know there's paperwork involved, but for someone already farming clean for 30 years, you might qualify easily.
I hear about VietGAP yes. but paperwork, you know. I am old man, not much school. and they want record of everything, water test, feed receipt. I keep some paper but not organized like they want

That's a very common barrier — the certification is designed for what you're already doing, but the documentation requirements can feel overwhelming, especially if paperwork isn't your strong suit.

Here's the thing though: you're already farming the right way. The certification wouldn't change how you farm — it would just prove it on paper. The gap is only the record-keeping, not the actual practices.

Some practical steps that might make it less daunting:

- **Start small** — just begin keeping a simple notebook: date, what you fed, how much, which pond. One line per day. That's the foundation of most records they want
- **Save receipts** — keep all feed purchase receipts in one envelope or folder
- **Phone photos** — if you have a smartphone, take photos of receipts, water conditions, anything notable. Dates are automatic
- **Ask for help** — sometimes local agricultural extension offices have staff who can help farmers with VietGAP applications. They walk you through the paperwork

Do you have a smartphone, or mostly use a basic phone?
I have phone yes. my son buy for me. I can take photo ok. but I am slow with typing, you know. these finger are for rope and net not for phone ha

Ha! Rope-and-net fingers trying to type on a tiny screen — I can picture that. But you're typing to me right now, so you're doing fine.

The photo method is actually perfect for you. Here's a simple daily system:

1. **Morning** — take one photo of each pond (shows water color and condition)
2. **When you feed** — take a photo of the feed bags with amount
3. **When you buy anything** — photo of the receipt before it fades or gets lost
4. **If anything unusual happens** — dead shrimp, water color change, anything — photo

That's it. No typing needed. The photos have dates and times built in. After a few months of this, you'd have a visual record that would cover most of what VietGAP asks for.

Your son who bought you the phone — does he live nearby? He might be able to help organize the photos into a simple folder system for you.

that is not bad idea friend. photo is easy. ok I think about it. maybe I talk to agriculture office in town. but you know these government people, they talk talk talk and nothing happen. I trust what I see with my own eye more than paper

I hear you — and honestly, your instinct has kept your ponds clean for 30 years, so it's clearly working. The certification is just a way to make other people see what you already know.

But there's no rush. The most important thing is that you keep farming the way your father taught you. Clean water, good feed, no shortcuts. That's what makes good shrimp, paper or no paper.

If you do visit the agriculture office, go in with specific questions: What exactly do I need? How long does it take? What does it cost? That way you control the conversation and they can't just talk in circles.

In the meantime, starting the photo habit costs nothing and takes 2 minutes a day. Even if you never apply for VietGAP, having a record of your ponds is useful for yourself — tracking water quality, feed amounts, seasonal patterns.

Your father's method has lasted two generations. That's worth more than any certificate.
yes that is true. two generation. ok friend thank you. I go check pond now, tide coming in and I need open gate. good talk today

Good timing with the tide! Go take care of your ponds. And if you feel like it, take a photo of the water when you open the gate — might as well start today.

Good talk. Take care, and keep farming the honest way.
